title: Suppressing the Motion of Rydberg Atoms in Inhomogeneous Electric Fields via Stark Echo
authors: Dominik Jakab, Manuel Kaiser, Conny Glaser, David Petrosyan, József Fortágh, Andreas Günther
abstract: Rydberg atoms possess strong electric dipole transitions and tunable energy levels, making them promising candidates for microwave to optical conversion on integrated superconducting atom chips. Achieving strong coupling of the atoms to e.g. the microwave field of an on-chip resonator requires placing the atoms within tens of micrometers from the chip surface. However, inhomogeneous stray electric fields originating from the surface can induce position-dependent Stark forces, resulting in atomic motion and leading to time-dependent shifts of the Rydberg energy levels. We experimentally investigate these effects using time-of-flight and spectroscopic techniques, observing substantial level shifts and signal loss attributable to field-induced atomic motion. A theoretical model incorporating an exponentially decaying surface field with a superimposed bias accurately reproduces the observed dynamics. To mitigate the level shift, we introduce a Stark echo sequence that dynamically reverses the force. This approach suppresses the atomic motion and maintains the atomic resonance. The method relies solely on global field control and is compatible with atom-resonator coupling architectures, providing a robust strategy for preserving coherence of Rydberg atoms in inhomogeneous electric fields near surfaces.
summary: The paper addresses the challenge of maintaining Rydberg atom coherence when trapped near imperfect superconducting surfaces due to inhomogeneous electric fields. By implementing a tailored Stark echo pulse sequence that dynamically reverses the force, the authors successfully suppress atomic motion and prevent detrimental time-dependent energy level shifts. This provides a robust method for realizing high-fidelity quantum operations on integrated atom chips.
main_problem: Inhomogeneous stray electric fields near superconducting atom chips induce position-dependent Stark forces on Rydberg atoms, causing atomic motion and time-dependent shifts in energy levels.
main_result: Implementing a Stark echo sequence dynamically reverses the force, effectively suppressing atomic motion and maintaining the coherence of Rydberg atoms for extended periods. This technique is robust and relies only on global field control.
method: The authors combine theoretical modeling (using an exponentially decaying surface field) with experimental measurements using time-of-flight spectroscopy to test and implement a pulsed Stark echo sequence.
model_or_system: The system involves Rydberg atoms trapped near a superconducting chip. The physics is governed by the position-dependent Stark shift, $E(\vec{E}) = - rac{1}{2}\alpha_0 E^2$, where the field $E(z)$ includes both surface and bias components.
key_observables: Average Rydberg level shift ($\langle\Delta\omega_rangle$), spatial confinement ($\langle z angle$ vs. TOF), and residual spectral broadening.
important_parameters: The decay length of the surface field ($\zeta$), the time-of-flight ($t_{	ext{TOF}}$ or $t_{	ext{echo}}$), and the strength of the applied bias field ($E_{	ext{bias}}$).
main_assumptions: The method relies solely on global field control, and hardware limitations constrain the minimum echo time to be greater than $\approx 3.4 \mu	ext{s}$.
figures_summary: Figures compare the mean level shift ($\langle\Delta\omega_rangle$) over time-of-flight with and without the echo sequence; results show near-complete suppression of the shift when the echo is applied.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the problem (field-induced motion/shift), develops a theoretical model incorporating surface fields, presents experimental measurements showing rapid decay/shifting without correction, and finally demonstrates that the Stark echo sequence successfully cancels these accumulated shifts.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial coherence preservation technique for using Rydberg atoms in solid-state quantum circuits. It shows how fundamental control over external fields can counteract environmental decoherence sources arising from device imperfections.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electric field and resulting Stark force near the chip surface. (a) Magnitude of the total electric field |E(z)| as a function of distance z from the chip surface, including the exponentially decaying surface field and the tunable bias. Red and blue lines correspond to Ebias = −4.02 V/cm and −12.93 V/cm, respectively. At the position of the atomic cloud (z0 = 25.75 µm), both values of the bias field produce the same total field magnitude (|Eres| = 4.45 V/cm), but with field gradients of opposite sign. The red shaded area illus- trates the spatial extent of the cloud. (b) Stark force F(z) experienced by 87Rb atoms in the 42S1/2 Rydberg state, cal- culated for the above field with...

FIG. 3. Schematics of the experiment. (a) A cloud of atoms (red) is magnetically trapped at a distance of z0 ≈ 25 µm below a superconducting atom chip. The atoms are located in a stray electric field (blue shaded gradient) de- caying exponentially with distance from the chip surface, Esur(z) = E0 exp(−z/ζ), and a homogeneous bias field Ebias (black dashed arrows) is generated by an electrode placed 4.5 mm below the chip surface, forming a plate capacitor with the chip. The inhomogeneous total electric field produces a position-dependent Stark force (red arrow), which accelerates the atoms and shifts the Rydberg energy level. (b) Pulse sequences for the three measurement protocols. Top: de-...

FIG. 2. Numerical simulation of Stark echo suppression of motional level shift. (a) Simulated center-of-mass position ⟨z⟩as a function of tTOF for atoms excited from an atomic cloud centered at z0 = 25.75 µm with temperature T = 1 µK. The solid black, blue and cyan lines show the results using Stark echo with techo = 1 µs, 2.5 µs and 5 µs, respectively. Due to the echo sequence, the atoms oscillate around their initial position, with the oscillation amplitude depending on techo. The dashed red line shows the trajectory without the echo sequence, with the atoms rapidly accelerating away from their initial position. (b) Corresponding mean level shift ⟨∆ωr⟩of 42S1/2 Rydberg atoms. The solid...

FIG. 4. De-excitation spectra of Rydberg atoms mea- sured for ∆ωexc/2π ≈150 MHz and different tTOF after excitation. Black circles: Spectrum for tTOF = 0, with an inverted Gaussian fit (solid line) yielding a detuning of ∆ωde-exc/2π = −1.4 MHz and a FWHM of 17.7 MHz. Red circle: Spectrum after tTOF = 4 µs, showing a pronounced red shift (∆ωde-exc/2π = −17.9 MHz) and spectral broaden- ing (FWHM = 28.7 MHz). Each data point is averaged over Nrep = 1000 pulse sequences. The error band denotes the 1σ confidence interval of the fit.

FIG. 6. Rydberg state population after given tTOF as de- duced from the total number of ion counts for Nrep = 1000 excitation-detection cycles and normalized to the tTOF = 0 value. Atoms are excited at a total field magnitude of |Eres| = 4.45 V/cm. Red: Field gradient accelerating atoms toward the chip, leading to rapid signal loss. Blue: Reversed field gradient accelerating atoms away from the chip and ex- tending detection time. Black: Application of the echo pulse sequence effectively cancels the Stark-induced position shift, preserving the Rydberg signal for durations approaching the natural lifetime. The solid lines are exponential decay fits yielding lifetimes of 5.92 µs, 47.12 µs and...
